content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 13,
1993
J.
Kenneth Brown
Cerritos City Attorney
Brown,
Winfield,
and Canzoneri
California Plaza
300 Grand Avenue,
Suite
1500
Los Angeles,
CA
90071—3125
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-159
Dear Mr.
Brown:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Victor Gotesman,
Theater Manager for the Cerritos Center
for the Performing Arts,
regarding his responsibilities under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) •1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
Are complimentary tickets made available to Mr.
Gotesman to
view potential performances for the Cerritos Center for the
Performing Arts “gifts” under the Act?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-159
Page
2
CONCLUSION
The free admission is considered “informational materials”
under the Act and is exempt from the definition of “gift.”
Thus
the tickets would not be subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act.
Transportation to and from various conferences and
performances,
and any other benefits received would not be
considered “informational material,” and would be gifts subject to
the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
FACTS
The Cerritos Center for the Performing Arts (the “Center”)
was inaugurated in January,
1993.
Mr.
Gotesman is the theater
manager and is charged with negotiating for all of the artistic
performances and the day—to—day management of the Center.
During the course of the year there are two major conferences
for agents and presenters.
You stated that at these conferences
agents, presenters,
and artists review and discuss potential
contracts for appearances.
On the basis of information gathered
at these conferences,
Mr.
Gotesinan schedules programs for the
coming season.
You stated that at these conferences it is customary that
major theaters make complimentary tickets available to theater
managers.
In keeping with the custom in the industry,
the Center
makes complimentary tickets available to other theater managers.
You also stated that it is not unusual for an agent to suggest
that Mr.
Gotesman see a performance to enable him to decide
whether the artist is appropriate for a Center appearance.
You
stated that the free tickets have no face value.
ANALYSIS
Gifts,
Generally
The Act was adopted by the voters of California by initiative
in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict—of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
every public official must
disclose all his or her economic interests that could foreseeably
be affected by the exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections
81002(c),
87200—87313.)
Section 87207 provides:


Our File No.
A-93—159
Page
3
(a)
When income is required to be reported
under this article, the statement shall contain...:
(1)
The name and address of each source
of income aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value,
or fifty
dollars
($50)
or more in value if the income
was a gift,
and a general description of the
business activity, if any,
of each source;
(2)
A statement whether the aggregate
value of income from each source,
or in the
case of a loan,
the highest amount owed to
each source,
was at least two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
but did not exceed one thousand
dollars
($1,000), whether it was in excess of
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
but was not
greater than ten thousand dollars
($10,000),
or whether it was greater than ten thousand
dollars
($10,000);
***
(4)
In the case of a gift, the amount
and the date on which the gift was received.
In addition,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public
official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision
in which the official has a financial interest.
2
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
3
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any
donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for a donor of,
a gift or
gifts aggregating
$250 or more in value provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.
2
The Act also limits the amount of gifts a local elected
official can receive.
(See,
Section 89501.)
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
A-93—159
Page
4
Section 82028(a)
defines a “gift” as:
(a)
“Gift” means, except as provided in
subdivision (b),
any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and includes a rebate or discount in the
price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business
to members of the public without regard to official
status.
Any person, other than a defendant in a
criminal action,
who claims that a payment is not a
gift by reason of receipt of consideration has the
burden of proving that the consideration received
is of equal or greater value.
(b)
The term “gift” does not include:
(1)
Informational material such as
books,
reports,
pamphlets, calendars,
or
periodicals.
No payment for travel or
reimbursement for any expenses shall be deemed
“informational material.”
(Emphasis added.)
“Informational material” means any item which
serves primarily to convey information and which is
provided to an official for the purpose of
assisting him or her in the performance of his or
her official duties.
Informational material may
include:
(c)
On—site demonstrations, tours,
or
inspections designed specifically for public
officials.
No payment for transportation to
an inspection, tour,
or demonstration site,
nor reimbursement for any expenses in
connection therewith,
shall be deemed
“informational material” except insofar as
such transportation is not commercially
obtainable.
Regulation 18942.1.
You stated that the theater manager of the Cerritos Center
for Performing Arts is required to arrange for performers at the
Center as part of his job duties.
Periodically, the theater
manager is provided free passes from agents to see performers that


Our File No.
A-93--159
Page
5
the manager may be considering for the Center.
This free admission
would constitute a disciosable gift under the Act unless there
exists an exception.
In 1975 the Commission interpreted “informational material”
to include both tangible and intangible materials.
Where a public
official was to receive a private guided tour of a nuclear power
plant,
the Commission found that the tour was the informational
material and therefore,
the value of the tour was not a gift to the
official.
(In re Speliman
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
16.)
We have also
advised that free admission to informational seminars may also be
considered “informational materials.”
(Newman Advice Letter,
No.
A—84—187.)
Mr.
Gotesman is the theater manager and is charged with
negotiating for all of the artistic performances and the day-to-day
management of the Center.
Since it is Mr.
Gotesman’s job to
evaluate the performers for the city,
we believe that with respect
to his unique position,
free admission to performances considered
for the center would be “informational material.”
4
Please note that any other benefits received in connection
with the performances,
such as food or beverages,
would be
considered gifts.
Additionally, transportation to and from various
conferences and performances would also be considered a gift.
(Regulation 18942.1(c).)
Finally,
free admission to events that
are unrelated to Mr.
Gotesman’s job duties would similarly be
considered a gift.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
,/
By:
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
We note that Mr.
Gotesman is in an unusual public position and
that this conclusion applies only to the person holding that
position with the City of Cerritos.
This conclusion is intended
to be a narrow one based on the facts presented and should not be
extended to any other person or situation.
